description: GLOMAP-mode is the Global Model of Aerosol Processes, a modal global aerosol microphysics model for UKCA. GLOMAP-mode simulates the multi-component global aerosol, resolving sulfate, sea-salt, dust, black carbon (BC) and particulate organic matter (POM), the latter including primary and biogenic secondary POM. Aerosol processes are simulated in a size-resolved manner including primary emissions, secondary particle formation by binary homogeneous nucleation of sulfuric acid and water, particle growth by coagulation, condensation and cloud-processing and removal by dry deposition, in-cloud and below-cloud scavenging.

The World Ocean Model of Biogeochemistry And Trophic-dynamics (WOMBAT) is based on a NPZD (nutrient–phytoplankton–zooplankton–detritus) model. The "lite" version of WOMBAT includes one class each of phytoplankton, zooplankton and sinking detritus, as well as nitrate (NO3), bio-available iron (Fe), dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC), calcium carbonate (CaCO3), alkalinity (ALK), and oxygen (O2). Fe is carried through the zooplankton and detrital pools as well. Gas exchange follows OCMIP2 protocols.

The **IFS atmospheric** component is based on a hydrostatic, semi-Lagrangian, semi-implicit dynamical core withcomputations alternated between spectral and reduced Gaussian grid-point representations at each time step. Thevertical discretization is based on a hybrid sigma-pressurecoordinate which is solved by using a finite-element scheme. Subgridscale processes are parameterized in terms of the resolvedvariables and include representations of a **Radiation Scheme** (A Rapid Radiative Transfer Model for shortwave and longwave radiation, including gas, cloud, and aerosol interactions, which is critical for accurate surface and atmospheric heating rates; [Morcrette et al., 2008](https://journals.ametsoc.org/view/journals/mwre/136/12/2008mwr2363.1.pdf)), a **Convection Scheme** (A mass-flux approach that represents deep and shallow convection, including convective momentum transport. It improves precipitation timing and intensity and is crucial for tropical and mid-latitude weather; [Bechtold et al., 2008](https://rmets.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/qj.289)), a **Cloud Microphysics Scheme** (A prognostic treatment of cloud liquid, ice, rain, snow, and graupel with detailed microphysical processes. It improves cloud cover and precipitation representation, including mixed-phase clouds; [Forbes and Tompkins, 2011](https://www.ecmwf.int/sites/default/files/elibrary/2011/17431-improved-representation-cloud-and-precipitation.pdf)), a **Turbulence and Boundary Layer Scheme** (Parameterizing the turbulent mixing in the planetary boundary layer, including non-local and local mixing processes. It influences the near-surface weather elements like temperature, humidity, and wind; [Köhler et al., 2011](https://doi.org/10.1002/qj.713)), subgrid-scale orographic drag ([Beljaars et al., 2004](https://rmets.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1256/qj.03.73)), and non-orographicgravity wave drag (Orr et al., 2010). See e.g. [Roberts et al.](https://gmd.copernicus.org/articles/11/3681/2018/) (2018, section 2.1).  The **Land Surface Scheme** in **HTESSEL** represents vertical transfers of water and energy using four vertical layers to represent soil temperature and moisture. The model evaluates the land surface response to the atmospheric forcing and estimates the surface water and energy fluxes along with the temporal evolution of the snowpack, soil temperature, and moisture. At the interface between the surface and the atmosphere, each grid box is divided into fractions (tiles), with up to six fractions over land (bare ground, low and high vegetation, intercepted water, shaded and exposed snow). Each fraction has its own properties defining separate heat and water fluxes used in the energy-balance equation solved for the tile skin temperature. The snow scheme in HTESSEL is an energy- and mass-balance model that represents an additional layer on top of the upper soil layer, with independent prognostic thermal and mass contents. See [Dutra et al.](https://doi.org/10.1175/2010JHM1249.1) (2010, section2).

JULES is the Joint UK Land Environment Simulator (Best et al. 2011) . This was developed from the Met Office Surface Exchange Scheme (MOSES). It can be used as a stand alone land surface model driven by observed forcing data, or coupled to an atmospheric global circulation model. The configuration used is documented by Willett et al 2026 for GA8 with changes in the calculation of surface albedo to improve agreement with observations and remove the previous scaling to climatology, and the soil hydrology parameterisation to improve drainage to lower soil layers (Rostron et al 2026)
references: https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-4-677-2011 (Best et al. 2011) https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-19-1473-2026 (Willett et al 2026) https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2026-1676 (Rostron et al 2026)

description: CLASS3.6/CTEM1.2 simulate the physical and biogeochemical land surface processes, respectively, and together they calculate fluxes of energy, water, CO2, and wetland CH4 emissions at the land–atmosphere boundary.

The Canadian Model of Ocean Carbon (CMOC) includes carbon chemistry and biology. The biological component is a simple nutrient–phytoplankton–zooplankton–detritus (NPZD) model, with fixed Redfield stoichiometry, and simple parameterizations of iron limitation, nitrogen fixation, and export flux of calcium carbonate. CMOC was migrated into the NEMO modelling system, and the following important modifications were made: (i) oxygen was added as a passive tracer with no feedback on biology; (ii) carbon chemistry routines were updated to conform to the OMIP-BGC protocol (Orr et al., 2017, https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-10-2169-2017); (iii) additional passive tracers requested by OMIP were added, including natural and abiotic dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) as well as the inert tracers (CFC11, CFC12, and SF6).

GAMIL3 is the atmospheric component of the FGOALS-g3.5 coupled climate system model, developed by the Institute of Atmospheric Physics (IAP), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS). 1.  Main processes simulated: - Global atmospheric circulation, including large-scale dynamics, and radiation processes. - Full treatment of moist convection, boundary layer processes, and cloud microphysics. 2.  Key physics/parameterizations: - Updated Zhang-McFarlane convection scheme with modifications for tropical convection. - Improved two-moment cloud microphysics and boundary layer turbulence scheme. - Hybrid sigma-pressure vertical coordinates with 26 levels up to ~2.194 hPa (42 km). 3.  Notable features: - Uses a 2.0° × 2.0° regular latitude-longitude grid in the horizontal. - Coupled to the CAS-LSM land model, LICOM3 ocean model, and Los Alamos sea ice model within FGOALS-g3.5. - Optimized for long-term climate simulations and CMIP7 experiments. - The freshwater exchange process between river model and the ocean model employs the nearest mapping method rather than the area conservative mapping method, as the former ensures that the ocean sufficiently receives the riverine freshwater input.

Main processes simulated (additions only) Within FESOM 2.7, FESIM 2.7 gains tighter coupling to an explicit iceberg drift-and-melt module (with wave-induced erosion), so that calved icebergs are treated as an external freshwater/heat source distinct from the resolved sea-ice cover. Consistent handling of land-ice and ice-shelf freshwater fluxes alongside sea-ice melt/growth is hardened, including the cold-start pathway used for transient passive tracers (CFCs, SF6, radiocarbon) and water isotopes (δ18O, δD) that interact with the ice–ocean interface.  Key physics / parameterisations (new or upgraded) Continued use of the modified elastic–viscous–plastic (mEVP) rheology on collocated triangular vertex meshes, with subcycle counts exposed via namelist. Flux-corrected transport advection of ice and snow mean thicknesses and concentration, unchanged in formulation but cleaned up at the interface to the restructured CVmix vertical-mixing layer of the host ocean. Improved ice–ocean–ice-shelf cavity consistency: rigid-lid/free-surface treatment under cavities, water-flux closure at the ice base, and a vertical-velocity reset that participates in the CFLz check. Calving/enthalpy logic reworked for stability under frequent restarts in coupled IFS-based configurations.  Notable features of this version First-phase OpenACC port: the FESIM dynamical kernels run alongside the FESOM 2.7 dynamical core on GPUs while retaining the existing MPI CPU path. CMIP7-oriented sea-ice diagnostics exposed via the ldiag_cmor diagnostic layer, with 0-dimensional global metrics written one file per simulated year. Asynchronous parallel sea-ice output through the MultIO 2.6 back-end, in addition to the native and XIOS paths. Coupled-system robustness improvements relevant to sea ice: corrected wind coupling at the ice surface, reproducibility under frequent restarts, and reduced coupling memory footprint. Build & test infrastructure: ecbundle support, multi-environment CI, and ctest regression tests that exercise cavity and iceberg setups together with the sea-ice component.

LIM is a numerical model of sea ice designed for climate studies and operational oceanography (https://www.cmcc.it/models/lim).  The LIM2 sea-ice model with parameters adjusted was used in CanESM5 (Swart et al. 2019, https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-12-4823-2019)

The CABLE model (Kowalczyk et al, 2006; Wang and Leuning, 1998) is embedded in the atmosphere and replaces most parts of the MOSES land model (the default land surface model in the UM7.3). Dust source, topographic drag and river routing remain as per MOSES.  Each grid-cell is divided into a variable number of vegetated (up to 13) and non-vegetated (up to 3) tiles. Each tile is independent and has its own soil column and up to three snow layers. Tile fractions are prescribed and change over time to account for land-use change. Compared to ACCESS-ESM1-5 (Ziehn et al., 2020), three new vegetated tiles are included (C4 crops and two Australian-specific tree types). Land biogeochemistry is included using the CASA-CNP module (Wang et al., 2010) simulating carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus cycles. Lakes are not explicitly simulated - lake tiles are permanently saturated soil columns with no vegetation. Wetlands, lake and ice tiles are invariant and grid-cells (primarily Antarctica and Greenland) are either completely ice or ice-free. CABLE code, choice of parameterisations and some parameter values have been updated relative to ACCESS-ESM1-5.
The chemistry scheme of CNRM-ESM2 is an on-line scheme whereby the chemistry routines are part of the physics of the atmospheric climate model.
name: REPROBUS-C (v2.0)

description: TACTIC (Tropospheric Aerosols for ClimaTe In CNRM) is an interactive tropospheric aerosol scheme, able to represent the main anthropogenic and natural aerosol types in the troposphere.
name: TACTIC

description: 1. Main processes simulated (additions only) Adds methane emissions from natural wetlands and peatlands as a standard feature, lifting them from earlier branch-only configurations into the main release. Emissions of nitrogen-based trace gases (N₂O, NO, NH₃) from soils are simulated in the coupled C–N cycle.  2. Key physics / parameterisations (new or upgraded) New process-based wildfire scheme as the default fire module, replacing the previous simpler default. Peatland-specific plant functional types and a dedicated peatland hydrology integrated into the main code path. Multi-layer soil temperature and freeze–thaw scheme available in the standard configuration, enabling permafrost dynamics outside the dedicated arctic branches. Soil nitrogen chemistry extended to support consistent trace-gas production alongside leaching and plant–microbial competition.  3. Notable features of this version First release of LPJ-GUESS, available directly from the project website. Consolidates previously branch-specific developments (high-latitude soil physics, methane, advanced fire) into a single, supported mainline code base.
UniCiCles (Unified Model-CISM-BISICLES) is a package combining BISICLES with an interface that obtains boundary conditions from Unified Model or JULES data.
name: BISICLES-UKESM-ISMIP6-1.0

description: The co2box v1.0 is a simple CO2 box model with just one global grid point.
references: A description paper for the EC-Earth3-ESM-1 model configuration is in preparation (submission medio 2026).

description: Pelagic Interaction Scheme for Carbon and Ecosystem Studies model volume 2 version trace gases.
name: PISCESv2-gas

description: The model represents atmospheric circulation and includes submodels for radiation, cloud microphysics, convection and boundary layer processes. The atmosphere configuration is as used for ACCESS-ESM1.5 (Ziehn et al., 2020) with a minor change to convective momentum transport and is similar to the atmosphere configuration of HADGEM2 (r1.0) (The HadGEM2 Development Team, 2011 ) but with the PC2 cloud scheme as for HADGEM2 (r1.1) (Hewitt et al, 2011).
Global Experimental Leads and sea ice for ATmosphere and Ocean.
name: GELATO

description: ARPEGE-Climat Version 6.3 is the atmospheric component of the CNRM climate and Earth System models (CNRM-CM6-1 and CNRM-ESM2-1).
name: Arpege-Climat Version 6.3

description: UK global sea-ice configuration of the SI3 model

The aerosol module is the Coupled Large-scale Aerosol Simulator for Studies In Climate (CLASSIC) as in HadGEM2 (Bellouin et al., 2011). It represents tropospheric aerosol species:  sulphate, mineral dust, sea salt, fossil fuel black carbon, fossil fuel organic carbon, biomass burning aerosols, secondary organic (also called biogenic) aerosols. Emissions of mineral dust and sea salt are interactive. Emissions for other species or their precursors are prescribed. Transported species experience boundary layer and convective mixing and are removed by dry and wet deposition.

1. Main processes simulated (additions only) Adds an explicit iceberg drift-and-melt module (with wave-induced erosion) that can run together with active ice-shelf cavities. Transient passive tracers (CFCs, SF6, radiocarbon) and water isotopes (δ18O, δD) are supported as production features, including consistent cold-start and land-ice flux handling.  2. Key physics / parameterisations (new or upgraded) Combined harmonic + biharmonic momentum viscosity as a single namelist-selectable closure. Dynamic kinetic-energy backscatter, including advection of the unresolved-eddy-kinetic-energy variable. Restructured CVmix interface, unified across vertical-mixing branches. Hardened ice-shelf cavity treatment: rigid-lid/free-surface consistency, water-flux closure, and vertical-velocity reset for the CFLz check. Updated global-tides module.  3. Notable features of this version First phase of OpenACC port: the dynamical core can run on GPUs alongside the existing MPI CPU path. CMIP7-oriented diagnostics via ldiag_cmor, producing 0-dimensional global diagnostics with one file per simulated year. MultIO 2.6 output back-end, providing asynchronous parallel I/O in addition to the native and XIOS paths. Coupled-system robustness for IFS-based configurations: reproducibility under frequent restarts, corrected wind coupling, reworked calving/enthalpy logic, and reduced coupling memory footprint. Build & test infrastructure: ecbundle support, separable mesh-partitioner build, multi-environment CI, ctest regression tests including cavity and iceberg setups, and auto-download of standard test meshes.

**Main processes simulated** OpenIFS 48r1 simulates the global three-dimensional atmospheric circulation together with its interactions with the land surface, sea ice and ocean wave fields. The model integrates the hydrostatic primitive equations on a terrain-following mass-based vertical coordinate, prognosing wind, temperature, specific humidity, cloud condensate (liquid, ice), precipitation species (rain, snow), cloud fraction and surface pressure. Coupled subsystems include the HTESSEL land-surface scheme (soil moisture and temperature, vegetation, snow, lakes via FLake) and the WAM ocean wave model. New at this cycle, OpenIFS 48r1 includes interactive prognostic ozone and consolidates OpenIFS and OpenIFS/AC into a single code base, with the operational CAMS chemistry and aerosol package available by default.  **Key physics and parameterizations** Cycle 48r1 represents a major upgrade of the moist physics package, restructuring the call sequence of the turbulence, shallow and deep convection, prognostic cloud and microphysics schemes for greater physical consistency. Notable changes include a new deep-convection closure with additional dependence on total advective moisture convergence; a change from exponential–exponential to random–exponential cloud vertical overlap; a vapour-deposition process for falling ice; and revised microphysics permitting supercooled drizzle drops to form and only freeze on contact with ice/snow or at much colder temperatures, enabling the prediction of freezing-drizzle events. Saturation adjustment and ice fall speed have been revised, and saturation-tendency perturbations have been removed from SPPT to suppress rare instabilities, while supersaturation adjustment has been added to ensemble SPPT perturbations with mass-weighting and a relaxation timescale. Radiation uses the ecRad scheme. The HTESSEL land surface is upgraded from a single-layer to a multi-layer snow scheme, markedly improving snowpack realism and the diurnal cycle of 2 m temperature in snow-covered regions. The dynamical core gains a new vertical finite-element discretisation, global mass fixers active for all moist species including water vapour, a revised semi-Lagrangian iterative trajectory solver, and cubic semi-Lagrangian departure-point interpolation with a 3-D quasi-monotone limiter for cloud and precipitation variables.  **Notable features of this version** Relative to the previous release (OpenIFS 43r3v2), 48r1 brings: (i) the major moist physics restructuring; (ii) the multi-layer snow scheme; (iii) interactive prognostic ozone; (iv) prediction of freezing drizzle; (v) improved water and energy conservation in the dynamics; (vi) consolidation of OpenIFS and OpenIFS/AC into a single code base with operational CAMS chemistry and aerosol available by default; (vii) a revised SPPT formulation; and (viii) updated climate fields (orography, land–sea mask, lake depth, glacier mask). Technical upgrades include migration from FCM to ecbuild/cmake, a major code restructure, single- and/or double-precision builds as default, inclusion of the Single Column Model (SCM) built alongside the 3-D model, and the ifs-test framework for known-good-output regression testing.
references: https://doi.org/10.21957/slk503fs2i https://doi.org/10.1175/JAS-D-13-0163.1 https://doi.org/10.1002/qj.713 https://doi.org/10.1175/2008JHM1068.1 https://doi.org/10.1029/2019MS001725 https://doi.org/10.1175/MWR-D-18-0043.1 https://doi.org/10.1256/qj.03.173 https://doi.org/10.21957/02054f0fbf

description: The Community Land Model represents several aspects of the land surface including surface heterogeneity through a nested subgrid hierarchy.
name: CLM4

description: Nucleus for European Modelling of the Ocean version 3.6 (OPA).
name: NEMO v3.6

description: CanAM5.1 (T63L49 native atmosphere, T63 Linear Gaussian Grid; 128 x 64 longitude/latitude; 49 levels; top level 1 hPa.  Aerosols are parameterized using a prognostic scheme for bulk concentrations of natural and anthropogenic aerosols, including sulfate, black and organic carbon, sea salt, and mineral dust; parameterizations for emissions, transport, gas-phase and aqueous-phase chemistry; and dry and wet deposition account for interactions with simulated meteorology.

CanNEMO introduces modifications to mesoscale and small-scale mixing physics to NEMO3.4.1 (ORCA1 tripolar grid, 1 deg with refinement to 1/3 deg within 20 degrees of the equator; 360 x 291 longitude/latitude; 45 vertical levels; top grid cell 0-6.19 m)

Version 5 of the Modular Ocean Model (MOM5, Griffies 2012) is a hydrostatic generalized level coordinate numerical ocean model with mass-conserving non-Boussinesq or volume-conserving Boussinesq kinematics on an Arakawa B-grid. ACCESS-ESM1.6 uses a Boussinesq configuration of MOM5 with a free surface and ‘z-star’ vertical coordinates which spread changes associated with the free surface throughout the water column. The grid resolution, bathymetry and parameterisations are unchanged from ACCESS-ESM1.5 (Ziehn et al., 2020).

Aerosols in CanAM5.1 are parameterized using a prognostic scheme for bulk concentrations of natural and anthropogenic aerosols, including sulfate, black and organic carbon, sea salt, and mineral dust; parameterizations for emissions, transport, gas-phase and aqueous-phase chemistry; and dry and wet deposition account for interactions with simulated meteorology.
SURFEXv8.0 encompasses several submodules for modeling the interactions between the atmosphere, the ocean, the lakes and the land surface.
name: SURFEX v8 modeling platform

description: UK physical global ocean configuration of the NEMO model

The Global Atmosphere 9 configuration of the Unified Model.  The main new developments in this configuration are: - The introduction of a prognostic-based convective entrainment rate (Willet et al., submitted). - Alignment of mid- and deep-level convection (with deep convection subsequently switched off). - A new parameterisation for riming (Furtado et al., 2017). - Bimodal cloud initiation (Weverberg et al., 2021a, b). - Several changes to improve the large-scale circulation (Williams et al., 2020).
references: "The Met Office Unified Model Global Atmosphere and Land 9.0 configuration" (Willet et al. in prep)

description: The ice model has a zero layer thermodynamic model that computes local growth rates of snow and sea ice due to vertical conductive, radiative and turbulent fluxes, along with snowfall on sea ice; the ice/snow surface temperature is controlled by the UM atmospheric component. The model represents the ice dynamics, which predicts the velocity field of the ice pack based on a model of the material strength of the ice;  and uses the EVP (elastic-viscous-plastic) rheology. An incremental remapping scheme is used for the advection of the areal concentration, sea ice volumes and other state variables; mechanical redistribution of the ice is carried out by a ridging parameterization that transfers sea ice among the five ice thickness categories based on energetic balances and rates of strain within the ice.
HadAM3 is the atmospheric component of HadCM3, a coupled atmosphere-ocean general circulation model developed at the UK Met Office Hadley Centre.
name: HadAM3
